answer: I can't give you a number right now — your resting heart rate is read from your wearable, and the data it provides is approximate, not exact. You have a 62 bpm reading, with HRV at 63 ms, and the trend is steady, so it's close to your usual 61.8 bpm, but I'd treat it as a best estimate rather than a precise measurement. If you want a real read, connect the watch or ring to your device overnight and log it again; then we can compare the two and say which is which.

Yes, it is. Your weekly strain average is 14.6, and today's 5.1 sits well above it, so you're absorbing yesterday's 96-minute ride with a load of 17.4 — a big effort — and still recovered enough to run a moderate session. The underlying reason is your HRV: 61 ms against a 30-day mean of 62.3, so your nervous system is absorbing the load rather than storing it. Keep the volume steady for now and the trend will likely return to your weekly average within a couple of weeks.

It was a normal session, not a typical one — your recovery came in at 63% despite a 7-day average of 66%, and your HRV of 60 ms is right on your 30-day mean of 61.4 ms. The likely driver is your training load, which has been trending around 12.1 over the past week, and yesterday's 83-minute run carried a hefty 15.9 load. So the session was workable, but not easy — keep the load where it is for the next couple of days and expect the score to rebound.

Last night was a bit short — 458 minutes, about 7.6 hours, against your 30-day average of 449.5 minutes, a difference of 10 minutes. The difference is small enough that your body is still absorbing yesterday's 41-minute strength session at a normal training load of 7.9 — a good sign. The likely reason is that your body was already carrying a small debt from the week, and a short night can't clear it entirely. If you have a late night tonight, make it a proper one rather than a sleep swap.

Two signals are down, and they're the ones that matter: your HRV is 58 ms, well below your 30-day average of 64 ms, and your resting heart rate is 63 bpm, up to 1.5 above its usual 60.4. Sleep is the only bright spot: 392 minutes, about 65 minutes over your weekly average of 447. The combination of a suppressed HRV and elevated resting heart rate is the classic signature of hard training, and it's the part that needs attention — hard training is good for the body, but it's brutal on the system, and a suppressed HRV after a hard session is the body's way of saying, "Stop pushing me, I can't handle it anymore." Your 7-day recovery average of 68% is also down, so the body is right. Protect sleep tonight and keep the first hard session of your planned week after that one. If HRV stays this low for several more days, that's worth a medical conversation.

63 ms — your wearable's estimate, not a doctor's. It sits right on your 7-day average of 65 ms, so you're in line with your usual pattern. Worth knowing: HRV is a lagging signal, so it reflects what happened a few days ago rather than a single training day. If you want a different read, keep logging your sleep and it will smooth over the weeks.

I can't give you a recovery score yet — your setup doesn't include one, and I'd rather not estimate or invent one. What I can see is your body: HRV is 66 ms against a 30-day average of 64.2, resting heart rate is 59 bpm versus a baseline of 59.8, and you slept 449 minutes, a solid night. Yesterday's 54-minute ride carried a hefty load of 10.9, so your body is still absorbing that. The score itself is calculated from overnight recovery, which I don't have, so treat this as a green light rather than a guarantee. Yes, you are breathing normally in your sleep. Your baseline respiratory rate is 15.2 bpm, well under your 30-day average of 15.4, and your 7-day average of 15.4 is only slightly above it, so there's no trend to worry about. The data doesn't include your sleep stages, so I can't say anything about the quality of your sleep, but the rate is the only signal I have, and it reads as expected. Your recovery is 70% and HRV at 62 ms is running above your 63.1 ms average, so you're in a good training state to absorb whatever sleep debt exists. If you want a more confident answer, a wearable that records both sleep and respiratory rate would give us the stage breakdown too.

Your HR is 66 bpm, and it's sitting right on your 7-day average of 61.8, so the number isn't rising — it's exactly the same. The likely driver is your sleep: 401 minutes, about 67 minutes, is a genuinely short night, and short sleep is the most common reason a resting heart rate stays elevated even when training load is low. It's benign and worth noting, not worrying about — a normal resting heart rate is 5.5 to 6.0 bpm for a man in your age band, so you're well under that. If the trend is rising, that's the thing to watch.

Recovery is a readiness number, 60% today, which is a good reading, and it's built from your body's own signals, not a formula. HRV is 61 ms, above your 30-day average of 62.6 ms, and resting heart rate is 60 bpm, right on your usual 60.2. The strain number is a strain scale, 0 to 21, and 12.4% is a small load, so you're moving well through it. The scale is different from a percentage, and it's meant to be read against your own history, not anyone else's. If you want a quick check, a strain of 21 or above is a warning, not a failure.

Yes, it was — 64 minutes against your weekly average of about 64, so it was exactly your norm, not a departure. The ride itself is worth noting too: 114 minutes at an average of 132 bpm, which is a genuinely hard effort, and your recovery of 67% says the body absorbed it well. A normal day after a hard ride like that is a full one, and a longer one than usual is the expected pattern.

Probably, but it's hard to tell from the data alone. Your HRV is 58 ms against a 7-day average of 63.9 ms, and the 7-day trend is suppressed, so the drop is real, but it's also the result of a short, hard night: 388 minutes of sleep against your usual 452, and a 49-minute run with a 10.4 load. The rest of your body is holding up: resting heart rate is 61 bpm, right on your 60.3 baseline, and recovery is 59% — a work day, not a training day. If sleep is the only thing that changes, the pattern will return within a week.
